* US: 414th * IA: [4th](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa")

([/suː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city in [Woodbury](/wiki/Woodbury_County,_Iowa "Woodbury County, Iowa") and [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth_County,_Iowa "Plymouth County, Iowa") counties in the U.S. state of [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"). The population was 85,797 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[4]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-4) and was estimated at 86,875 in 2024,[[3]](#cite_note-USCensusEst2024-3) making it the [fourth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa") in Iowa. The [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Woodbury County, Sioux City is the primary city of the five-county [Sioux City metropolitan area](/wiki/Sioux_City_metropolitan_area "Sioux City metropolitan area"), which had 144,334 residents on the 2020 census. Sioux City and the surrounding areas of northwestern Iowa, northeastern [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") and southeastern [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") are sometimes referred to collectively as [Siouxland](/wiki/Siouxland "Siouxland").

**Sioux City** ([/suː/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) is a city in [Woodbury](/wiki/Woodbury_County,_Iowa "Woodbury County, Iowa") and [Plymouth](/wiki/Plymouth_County,_Iowa "Plymouth County, Iowa") counties in the U.S. state of [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"). The population was 85,797 at the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"),[[4]](#cite_note-2020_Census_(City)-4) and was estimated at 86,875 in 2024,[[3]](#cite_note-USCensusEst2024-3) making it the [fourth-most populous city](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Iowa "List of cities in Iowa") in Iowa. The [county seat](/wiki/County_seat "County seat") of Woodbury County, Sioux City is the primary city of the five-county [Sioux City metropolitan area](/wiki/Sioux_City_metropolitan_area "Sioux City metropolitan area"), which had 144,334 residents on the 2020 census. Sioux City and the surrounding areas of northwestern Iowa, northeastern [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") and southeastern [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") are sometimes referred to collectively as [Siouxland](/wiki/Siouxland "Siouxland").

Sioux City is located at the navigational head of the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River"). The city is home to several cultural points of interest including the [Sioux City Public Museum](/wiki/Sioux_City_Public_Museum "Sioux City Public Museum"), [Sioux City Art Center](/wiki/Sioux_City_Art_Center "Sioux City Art Center") and [Sergeant Floyd Monument](/wiki/Sergeant_Floyd_Monument "Sergeant Floyd Monument"), which is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"). The city is also home to Chris Larsen Park, commonly referred to as "the Riverfront", which includes the Anderson Dance Pavilion, [Sergeant Floyd River Museum & Welcome Center](/wiki/Sergeant_Floyd_(towboat) "Sergeant Floyd (towboat)") and Lewis and Clark Interpretive Center.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Sioux_City%2C_Iowa_water_front_scene_-_NARA_-_285906.tif/lossy-page1-250px-Sioux_City%2C_Iowa_water_front_scene_-_NARA_-_285906.tif.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sioux_City,_Iowa_water_front_scene_-_NARA_-_285906.tif)

Waterfront, c. 1912

Main article: [History of Sioux City, Iowa](/wiki/History_of_Sioux_City,_Iowa "History of Sioux City, Iowa")

[Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa") is in the [tallgrass prairie](/wiki/Tallgrass_prairie "Tallgrass prairie") of the North American [Great Plains](/wiki/Great_Plains "Great Plains"), historically inhabited by speakers of [Siouan languages](/wiki/Siouan_languages "Siouan languages").
The area of Sioux City was inhabited by [Yankton](/wiki/Dakota_people "Dakota people") [Sioux](/wiki/Sioux "Sioux") when it was first reached by Spanish and French [furtrappers](/wiki/Fur_trade "Fur trade") in the 18th century. The first documented US citizens to record their travels through this area were [Meriwether Lewis](/wiki/Meriwether_Lewis "Meriwether Lewis") and [William Clark](/wiki/William_Clark "William Clark") during the summer of 1804. Sergeant [Charles Floyd](/wiki/Charles_Floyd_(explorer) "Charles Floyd (explorer)"), a member of the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition"), died here on August 20, 1804, the only death during the two and a half-year expedition.[[7]](#cite_note-LCHistory-7)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/1859_nebraska.jpg/250px-1859_nebraska.jpg)](/wiki/File:1859_nebraska.jpg)

1859 map of route from Sioux City, Iowa, through Nebraska, to gold fields of Wyoming, partially following old Mormon trails

Sioux City was laid out in the winter of 1854–1855.[[8]](#cite_note-8) It became a major transportation hub to the western Plains, including Mormons heading to Salt Lake City and speculators heading to Wyoming goldfields.

In 1891, the Sioux City Elevated Railway was opened and became the third steam-powered elevated [rapid transit](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") system in the world, and later the first electric-powered elevated railway in the world after conversion in 1892. However, the system fell into bankruptcy and closed within a decade.[[9]](#cite_note-Elevated_Railway-9)

The city gained the nickname "Little Chicago" during the [Prohibition](/wiki/Prohibition_in_the_United_States "Prohibition in the United States") era due to its reputation for being a purveyor of alcoholic beverages.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

On July 19, 1989, [United Airlines Flight 232](/wiki/United_Airlines_Flight_232 "United Airlines Flight 232") crash-landed at [Sioux Gateway Airport](/wiki/Sioux_Gateway_Airport "Sioux Gateway Airport"). 112 people on board the aircraft were killed, but 184 survived the crash and ensuing fire due to outstandingly quick performances by fire and emergency local teams.[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

According to a 2015 University of Iowa study for the Iowa Initiative for Sustainable Communities,[[13]](#cite_note-13) blight and disinvestment are serious problems in the downtown core as investment has shifted to suburbs.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

Sioux City borders two states, [South Dakota](/wiki/South_Dakota "South Dakota") to the west-northwest and [Nebraska](/wiki/Nebraska "Nebraska") to the west.

According to the [United States Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), the city has a total area of 60.556 square miles (156.84 km2), of which 59.387 square miles (153.81 km2) is land and 1.169 square miles (3.03 km2) (1.93%) is water.[[2]](#cite_note-CenPopGazetteer2025-2)

### Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Climate")]

As is typical of Iowa, Sioux City has a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate"), with very warm, humid summers, cold, dry winters, and wide temperature extremes; it is part of USDA [Hardiness zone](/wiki/Hardiness_zone "Hardiness zone") 5a.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The normal monthly mean temperature ranges from 20.0 °F (−6.7 °C) in January to 74.2 °F (23.4 °C) in July. On average, there are 25 days that reach 90 °F (32 °C) or higher, 52 days that do not climb above freezing, and 17 days with a low of 0 °F (−18 °C) or below annually. The average window for freezing temperatures is October 1 through April 26, allowing a growing season of 157 days. Extreme temperatures officially range from −35 °F (−37 °C) on January 12, 1912 up to 111 °F (44 °C) on July 4, 1936 and July 17, 1936, as well as July 11, 1939; the record cold daily maximum is −22 °F (−30 °C) on February 8, 1899, while, conversely, the record warm daily minimum is 86 °F (30 °C) on August 18, 1936. On 14 May 2013, the high temperature reached 106 °F (41 °C), setting a new all-time May record high, along with a 77 °F (43 °C) rise from the morning of the 12th, just two weeks after the city’s first measurable snowfall (1.6 inches (4.1 cm) on May 1) recorded in the month of May.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Precipitation is greatest in May and June and averages 29.27 inches (743 mm) annually but has ranged from 14.33 inches (364 mm) in 1976 to 41.10 inches (1,044 mm) in 1903. Snowfall averages 36.0 inches (91 cm) per season, and has historically ranged from 6.9 inches (18 cm) in 1895–1896 to 65.9 inches (167 cm) in 1961–1962; the average window for measurable (≥0.1 inches or 0.25 centimetres) snowfall is November 8 through April 7, although snow in October occurs several times per decade.

According to realtor website [Zillow](/wiki/Zillow "Zillow"), the average price of a home as of October 31, 2025, in Sioux City is $193,736.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

As of the 2024 [American Community Survey](/wiki/American_Community_Survey "American Community Survey"), there are 32,711 estimated households in Sioux City with an average of 2.59 persons per household. ### 2020 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2020 census")]

As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), there were 85,797 people, 32,170 households, and 20,217 families residing in the city.[[27]](#cite_note-27) The [population density](/wiki/Population_density "Population density") was 1,467.62 inhabitants per square mile (566.7/km2). There were 34,331 housing units at an average density of 587.26 per square mile (226.7/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 67.97% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 5.88% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 2.78% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 3.27% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.75% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), 9.21% from some other races and 10.15% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") people of any race were 20.93% of the population.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

The city has significant minority populations of West Africans, Somalis, Ethiopians, Vietnamese, Mexicans, and Guatemalans. This has been attributed to the many meat factories and manufacturing jobs in the area.

### 2010 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2010 census")]

As of the [2010 census](/wiki/2010_United_States_census "2010 United States census"), there were 82,684 people, 31,571 households, and 20,144 families residing in the city. The population density was 1,441.74 inhabitants per square mile (556.7/km2). There were 33,425 housing units at an average density of 582.82 per square mile (225.0/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 80.60% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 2.87% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 2.58% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 2.73% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.12% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), 7.38% from some other races and 3.72% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") people of any race were 16.45% of the population.

There were 31,571 households, of which 34.3% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 44.2% were [married couples](/wiki/Marriage "Marriage") living together, 13.8% had a female householder with no husband present, 5.9% had a male householder with no wife present, and 36.2% were non-families. 29.4% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.1% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.54 and the average family size was 3.14.

The median age in the city was 33.7 years. 26.6% of residents were under the age of 18; 11.4% were between the ages of 18 and 24; 25.6% were from 25 to 44; 24% were from 45 to 64, and 12.4% were 65 years of age or older. The gender makeup of the city was 49.2% male and 50.8% female.

### 2000 census

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2000 census")]

As of the [2000 census](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census"), there were 85,013 people, 32,054 households, and 21,091 families residing in the city. The population density was 1,551.30 inhabitants per square mile (599.0/km2). There were 33,816 housing units at an average density of 617.07 per square mile (238.3/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 85.23% [White](/wiki/White_(U.S._Census) "White (U.S. Census)"), 2.41% [African American](/wiki/African_American_(U.S._Census) "African American (U.S. Census)"), 1.95% [Native American](/wiki/Native_American_(U.S._Census) "Native American (U.S. Census)"), 2.82% [Asian](/wiki/Asian_(U.S._Census) "Asian (U.S. Census)"), 0.04% [Pacific Islander](/wiki/Race_(U.S._Census) "Race (U.S. Census)"), 5.27% from some other races and 2.28% from two or more races. [Hispanic or Latino](/wiki/Hispanic_and_Latino_Americans "Hispanic and Latino Americans") people of any race were 10.89% of the population.

There were 32,054 households, of which 33.4% had children under the age of 18 living with them, 49.1% were married couples living together, 12.2% had a female householder with no husband present, and 34.2% were non-families. 27.7% of all households were made up of individuals, and 11.3% had someone living alone who was 65 years of age or older. The average household size was 2.57 and the average family size was 3.14.

Age spread: 27.1% under the age of 18, 11.0% from 18 to 24, 28.5% from 25 to 44, 20.2% from 45 to 64, and 13.3% who were 65 years of age or older. The median age was 33 years. For every 100 females, there were 95.4 males. For every 100 females age 18 and over, there were 92.2 males.

The median income for a household in the city was $37,429, and the median income for a family was $45,751. Males had a median income of $31,385 versus $22,470 for females. The [per capita income](/wiki/Per_capita_income "Per capita income") for the city was $18,666. About 7.9% of families and 11.2% of the population were below the [poverty line](/wiki/Poverty_threshold "Poverty threshold"), including 15.0% of those under age 18 and 7.8% of those age 65 or over.

### Metropolitan area

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Metropolitan area")]

As of the 2020 census, the [Sioux City metropolitan area](/wiki/Sioux_City_metropolitan_area "Sioux City metropolitan area") had 144,334 residents in four counties. As defined by the [Office of Management and Budget](/wiki/Office_of_Management_and_Budget "Office of Management and Budget"), the counties comprising the metropolitan area are (in descending order of population):

* [Woodbury County, Iowa](/wiki/Woodbury_County,_Iowa "Woodbury County, Iowa")

### Crime

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Crime")]

Sioux City has a crime rate that is 91% higher than the average for Iowa and 63% higher than the national average. The violent crime rate is 90% above the Iowa average and 49% higher than the national average, based on the FBI's uniform crime reports for 2020.[[29]](#cite_note-29) According to the report, this represented a 12% decrease over the prior year.

## Arts and culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Arts and culture")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b6/091607-SiouxCity-Historic4th.jpg/250px-091607-SiouxCity-Historic4th.jpg)](/wiki/File:091607-SiouxCity-Historic4th.jpg)

[Fourth Street Historic District](/wiki/Fourth_Street_Historic_District_(Sioux_City,_Iowa) "Fourth Street Historic District (Sioux City, Iowa)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dc/Hard_Rock_Hotel_%26_Casino_Sioux_City%2C_IA.jpg/250px-Hard_Rock_Hotel_%26_Casino_Sioux_City%2C_IA.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hard_Rock_Hotel_%26_Casino_Sioux_City,_IA.jpg)

[Hard Rock Hotel and Casino](/wiki/Hard_Rock_Hotel_and_Casino "Hard Rock Hotel and Casino")

* The [Sioux City Public Museum](/wiki/Sioux_City_Public_Museum "Sioux City Public Museum") was originally located in a Northside neighborhood of fine Victorian mansions. The portico-and-gabled stone building was originally the home of the banker, John Peirce, and was built in 1890. The museum was recently relocated to downtown Sioux City, where it features Native American, pioneer, early Sioux City, and natural history exhibits.
* The [Sioux City Art Center](/wiki/Sioux_City_Art_Center "Sioux City Art Center"), located Downtown, was formed in 1938 as part of the [WPA](/wiki/Works_Progress_Administration "Works Progress Administration")'s support of the arts.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The Art Center supports artists from Iowa and the greater Midwest. Also, the Center has a general program of acquisition of work by national and international artists, including important works by [Thomas Hart Benton](/wiki/Thomas_Hart_Benton_(painter) "Thomas Hart Benton (painter)"), [Salvador Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí"), [Käthe Kollwitz](/wiki/K%C3%A4the_Kollwitz "Käthe Kollwitz"), [Robert Motherwell](/wiki/Robert_Motherwell "Robert Motherwell"), [Claes Oldenburg](/wiki/Claes_Oldenburg "Claes Oldenburg"), [James Abbott McNeill Whistler](/wiki/James_Abbott_McNeill_Whistler "James Abbott McNeill Whistler"), and [Grant Wood](/wiki/Grant_Wood "Grant Wood").
* The [Sergeant Floyd Monument](/wiki/Sergeant_Floyd_Monument "Sergeant Floyd Monument") commemorates the burial site of U.S. Army Sergeant [Charles Floyd](/wiki/Charles_Floyd_(explorer) "Charles Floyd (explorer)"), the only man to die on the [Lewis and Clark Expedition](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Expedition "Lewis and Clark Expedition").[[7]](#cite_note-LCHistory-7) It is a [National Historic Landmark](/wiki/National_Historic_Landmark "National Historic Landmark"), with its prominent 100 ft (30 m) obelisk situated on 23 acres (9.3 ha) of parkland, high on a river bluff with a view of the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River") valley.
* Chris Larsen Park, informally known as "The Riverfront," includes the Anderson Dance Pavilion, the Sergeant Floyd Riverboat Museum and the Lewis and Clark Interpretive Center, opened in 2004. Missouri River development began in 2005 with the opening of the MLR Tyme Marina area, which included Bev's on the River, an upscale restaurant, that has now become a Crave.[[32]](#cite_note-32)
* The [Sioux City Symphony Orchestra](/wiki/Sioux_City_Symphony_Orchestra "Sioux City Symphony Orchestra") (SCSO) was founded in 1915. The orchestra continues offering seven concerts within its annual season. Performances take place in the [Orpheum Theatre](/wiki/Orpheum_Theatre_(Sioux_City,_Iowa) "Orpheum Theatre (Sioux City, Iowa)") in Sioux City, Iowa. Concert dates run from September to April each year. The SCSO has included several movie scores, with film, on its concert schedule. The SCSO's education programming reaches 9,000 to 12,000 young people via the partnership with Carnegie Hall's Link Up program with 100 orchestras in the country, programs for SCSO musicians to perform and teach music lessons in the schools, and performances in nursing homes, hospitals, and elsewhere.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Milwaukee Railroad Shop is a 31.5 acres (12.7 ha) facility that is being renovated by the Siouxland Historical Railroad Association. It includes a [4-6-2 Pacific type](/wiki/4-6-2 "4-6-2") steam locomotive, the [Great Northern 1355](/wiki/Great_Northern_1355 "Great Northern 1355"), a model railroad exhibit, as well as multiple buildings including the roundhouse that are open to the public.
* Grandview Park is located north of the downtown area, up from Rose Hill, between The Northside and The Heights. The Municipal Bandshell is located in the park with Sunday evening municipal band concerts. The [Saturday in the Park](/wiki/Saturday_in_the_Park_(music_festival) "Saturday in the Park (music festival)") music festival began in 1991 and is held there annually on a weekend close to the Fourth of July holiday. Behind the bandshell is a rose garden with an arbor and trellises which has been a site for outdoor weddings, prom and other special occasion photographs, and for children to play during the Sunday evening band concerts and other events. Downtown is also home to the historic [Orpheum Theatre](/wiki/Orpheum_Theatre_(Sioux_City,_Iowa) "Orpheum Theatre (Sioux City, Iowa)"). In 1927 when it was built, it was the largest theater in Iowa.[[33]](#cite_note-33)
* Theatre is produced in Sioux City by two main entities, the Sioux City Community Theatre (SCCT), and LAMB Arts Regional Theatre. Each of these produce a full season of shows each year.

## Sports

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Sports")]

* The [Sioux City Bandits](/wiki/Sioux_City_Bandits "Sioux City Bandits") are an [indoor football](/wiki/Indoor_American_football "Indoor American football") team that play in [National Arena League](/wiki/National_Arena_League "National Arena League"). The Bandits play their home games at the [Tyson Events Center](/wiki/Tyson_Events_Center "Tyson Events Center").
* The [Sioux City Explorers](/wiki/Sioux_City_Explorers "Sioux City Explorers") are an [independent baseball](/wiki/Independent_baseball "Independent baseball") team playing in the [American Association](/wiki/American_Association_of_Independent_Professional_Baseball "American Association of Independent Professional Baseball"). The Explorers play their home games at [Lewis and Clark Park](/wiki/Lewis_and_Clark_Park "Lewis and Clark Park"). They have been to the league playoffs five times.
* The [Sioux City Musketeers](/wiki/Sioux_City_Musketeers "Sioux City Musketeers") are a junior hockey team based in Sioux City. They play in the [United States Hockey League](/wiki/United_States_Hockey_League "United States Hockey League") (USHL). They play their home games at the [Tyson Events Center](/wiki/Tyson_Events_Center "Tyson Events Center"). Their first year of hockey was in 1972. The Musketeers have won the gold cup in the 1985–1986 season, the National Runner-up twice (1993–94, 1995–96), the Anderson Cup three times (1981–82, 1985–86, 2016–17), the Clark Cup four times (1981–82, 1985–86, 2001–02, 2021- 22), and Western Division Champions for the 2004–05, and 2016–17 seasons.
* The [Sioux City Roller Dames](/wiki/Sioux_City_Roller_Dames "Sioux City Roller Dames") were a non-profit roller derby corporation. The Roller Dames played all home games at the [Longlines Family Recreation Center](/wiki/Sioux_City_Municipal_Auditorium "Sioux City Municipal Auditorium"). The Dames hosted their first tournament in November 2008 and dissolved in December 2016.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)
* In the late 19th century, the [Sioux City Cornhuskers](/wiki/Sioux_City_Cornhuskers "Sioux City Cornhuskers") played baseball in the [Western League](/wiki/Western_League_(original) "Western League (original)") before relocating. After a [five-year stint](/wiki/St._Paul_Saints#St._Paul_Saints_(1894–1899) "St. Paul Saints") in [St. Paul, Minnesota](/wiki/St._Paul,_Minnesota "St. Paul, Minnesota") as the [St. Paul Saints](/wiki/St._Paul_Saints_(1884%E2%80%931900) "St. Paul Saints (1884–1900)"), the league changed its name to the [American League](/wiki/American_League "American League"), and the team moved to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago"), where it continues today as the [Chicago White Sox](/wiki/Chicago_White_Sox "Chicago White Sox").
* The Sioux City Stampede play amateur outdoor football in the Midwest Football Alliance.
* The Sioux City Swine plays rugby union.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Stoneparkwoods.jpg/250px-Stoneparkwoods.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stoneparkwoods.jpg)

Stone State Park

* [Stone State Park](/wiki/Stone_State_Park "Stone State Park") is in the northwest corner of the city, overlooking the South Dakota/Iowa border. Stone Park is near the northernmost extent of the [Loess Hills](/wiki/Loess_Hills "Loess Hills"), and is at the transition from clay bluffs and prairie to sedimentary rock hills and [bur oak](/wiki/Bur_oak "Bur oak") forest along the Iowa side of the [Big Sioux River](/wiki/Big_Sioux_River "Big Sioux River"). The park is used by [picnickers](/wiki/Picnic "Picnic"), day hikers, and for mountain biking.
* Dorothy Pecaut Nature Center is a destination nature preserve for Woodbury County, and is located within the boundaries of Stone State Park. The butterfly garden is unique to the area; [wild turkeys](/wiki/Wild_turkey "Wild turkey") and [white-tail deer](/wiki/White-tail_deer "White-tail deer") are commonly sighted from the well-marked trails.
* Downtown entertainment venues include the Hard Rock Hotel & Casino, the 10,000-seat [Tyson Events Center](/wiki/Tyson_Events_Center "Tyson Events Center")/ Fleet Farm Arena, [Sioux City Orpheum Theatre](/wiki/Orpheum_Theatre_(Sioux_City,_Iowa) "Orpheum Theatre (Sioux City, Iowa)"), Promenade Cinema 14 and the Anderson Dance Pavilion which overlooks the [Missouri River](/wiki/Missouri_River "Missouri River").
* Pulaski Park is named for the Polish General [Kazimierz Pułaski](/wiki/Kazimierz_Pu%C5%82aski "Kazimierz Pułaski"), who fought in the American Revolution. This park features baseball diamond facilities, and is located in western Morningside along old U.S. Highway 75 (South Lewis Blvd.). It is largely built on the filled lakebed of Half Moon Lake, which was originally created in the 1890s by the excavation of fill dirt to build the approaches for the iron railroad bridge spanning the Missouri near the stockyards. The neighborhood on the bluff overlooking the park was historically settled by [Lithuanian](/wiki/Lithuanians "Lithuanians") and Polish immigrants, many of whom worked in the meatpacking industry during the early 20th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Latham Park is located in a residential area of Morningside, and is the only privately owned and maintained open-to-the-public park within the city limits.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It was left in trust in 1937 under the terms of Clara Latham's will; her family had built the house on 1-acre (4,000 m2) of ground in 1915. The house and grounds are currently being restored by the Friends of Latham Park.
* First Bride's Grave is tucked in a corner pocket of South Ravine Park, lies a series of paths, trails, and steps leading to the grave of the First Bride of Sioux City, Rosalie Menard. She was the first bride of a non-Native American to be wed in Sioux City, Iowa, thus receiving her title.
* War Eagle Park is named for the [Yankton Sioux](/wiki/Yankton_Sioux "Yankton Sioux") chief [Wambdi Okicize](/wiki/Chief_War_Eagle "Chief War Eagle") (d. 1851) who befriended early settlers. A monument overlooks the confluence of the Big Sioux and Missouri Rivers. The sculpture represents the chief in his role as a leader and peacemaker, wearing the [eagle feather bonnet](/wiki/War_bonnet "War bonnet") and holding the [ceremonial pipe](/wiki/Ceremonial_pipe "Ceremonial pipe").
* Riverside Park is located on the banks of the Big Sioux River. One of the oldest recreational areas of the city, it is home to the Sioux City Boat Club and Sioux City Community Theater. The park is on land that once belonged to the first white settler in the area, [Théophile Bruguier](/wiki/Th%C3%A9ophile_Bruguier "Théophile Bruguier"); his original cabin is preserved in the park.[[36]](#cite_note-36)
* Bacon Creek Park is located northeast of Morningside and features a scenic walking trail, dog park, picnic shelters, and playground equipment.

Golf courses, city parks, and aquatics: Sioux City is also home to several municipal public [golf courses](/wiki/Golf_course "Golf course"), including Floyd Park in Morningside, Green Valley near the Southern Hills, Sun Valley on the northern West Side, and Hidden Acres in nearby Plymouth County. Sioux City also has a number of private golf clubs, including Sioux City Country Club, and Whispering Creek Golf Club. The city has over 1,132 acres (5 km2) of public parkland located at 53 locations, including the riverfront and many miles of recreation trails. Five public swimming pools/aquatics centers are located within Sioux City neighborhoods.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Education")]

### Public schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Public schools")]

The [Sioux City Community School District](/wiki/Sioux_City_Community_School_District "Sioux City Community School District") served 14,569 students in the 2018–2019 school year;[[37]](#cite_note-37) there are three public high schools [West High School](/wiki/West_High_School_(Sioux_City,_Iowa) "West High School (Sioux City, Iowa)"), [North High School](/wiki/Sioux_City_North_High_School "Sioux City North High School"), [East High School](/wiki/East_High_School_(Sioux_City,_Iowa) "East High School (Sioux City, Iowa)") (grades 9-12), three public Middle Schools, West Middle, North Middle, and East Middle (grades 6-8), and 19 Elementary Schools (grades K-5).[[38]](#cite_note-38)

Because of sprawl, districts around Sioux City continue to grow at dramatic rates. [South Sioux City](/wiki/South_Sioux_City,_Nebraska "South Sioux City, Nebraska"), [Hinton](/wiki/Hinton,_Iowa "Hinton, Iowa"), [North Sioux City](/wiki/North_Sioux_City,_South_Dakota "North Sioux City, South Dakota"), [Lawton](/wiki/Lawton,_Iowa "Lawton, Iowa"), [Bronson](/wiki/Bronson,_Iowa "Bronson, Iowa"), [Elk Point](/wiki/Elk_Point,_South_Dakota "Elk Point, South Dakota"), [Jefferson](/wiki/Jefferson,_South_Dakota "Jefferson, South Dakota"), [Vermillion](/wiki/Vermillion,_South_Dakota "Vermillion, South Dakota"), [Le Mars](/wiki/Le_Mars,_Iowa "Le Mars, Iowa"), [Hawarden](/wiki/Hawarden,_Iowa "Hawarden, Iowa"), [Akron](/wiki/Akron,_Iowa "Akron, Iowa"), [Westfield](/wiki/Westfield,_Iowa "Westfield, Iowa"), [Ponca](/wiki/Ponca,_Nebraska "Ponca, Nebraska"), [Sergeant Bluff](/wiki/Sergeant_Bluff,_Iowa "Sergeant Bluff, Iowa"), [Wayne](/wiki/Wayne,_Nebraska "Wayne, Nebraska"), [Sioux Center](/wiki/Sioux_Center,_Iowa "Sioux Center, Iowa"), along with other school districts that serve many metro-area students.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Private schools")]

[Bishop Heelan Catholic Schools](/wiki/Bishop_Heelan_Catholic_High_School "Bishop Heelan Catholic High School") is a centralized private Catholic School System that includes six schools: They teach preschool through twelfth grade.

#### Air

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Air")]

The city is served by [Sioux Gateway Airport](/wiki/Sioux_Gateway_Airport "Sioux Gateway Airport") (SUX) 6 mi (9.7 km) to its south where [United Airlines](/wiki/United_Airlines "United Airlines")' affiliate [SkyWest Airlines](/wiki/SkyWest_Airlines "SkyWest Airlines") has announced it plans to discontinue the one flight per day each to [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") and [Denver](/wiki/Denver "Denver") it currently offers. As those flights are federally subsidized under the [Essential Air Service](/wiki/Essential_Air_Service "Essential Air Service") program, SkyWest is required to continue those flights until a replacement is found.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

[FBO](/wiki/Fixed-base_operator "Fixed-base operator") and jet charter services are currently offered by Ascension FBO Network.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

#### Other transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Sioux_City,_Iowa&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Other transportation")]

[Jefferson Lines](/wiki/Jefferson_Lines "Jefferson Lines") runs long-distance bus routes to Sioux City. Non-Transfer destinations include [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), [Kansas City](/wiki/Kansas_City,_Missouri "Kansas City, Missouri"), [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), and [Omaha](/wiki/Omaha,_Nebraska "Omaha, Nebraska").

Sioux City also has several private taxi companies that operate within the city.

There is no established water or rail passenger transportation in the area. The last passenger train was the [Illinois Central](/wiki/Illinois_Central "Illinois Central")'s *Hawkeye,* a daily train to Chicago via Waterloo, Dubuque and Rockford, discontinued in 1971.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Big Soo Terminal offers barge transportation.[[45]](#cite_note-45)
